title: Cohort Retention vs Monthly Churn at Low MRR: What Actually Matters
meta_description: Monthly churn is a vanity number under $20k MRR. Here's why cohort retention tells the truth — and what I wish I'd tracked from month one.
h1: I Watched My Churn Rate for 14 Months and Learned Nothing
body_markdown:
In March 2023, I was staring at a churn rate of 3.2% and convinced I had product-market fit. I had 47 paying customers at $39/mo. Do the math — that's one cancellation flipping my "churn rate" by more than two full percentage points. One guy in Lisbon canceling because his side project died was enough to make my metrics look either world-class or apocalyptic depending on the week.

I spent 14 months tracking monthly churn as my north star. It told me nothing useful.

What finally moved the needle — and what I'd tell any founder under $20k MRR to do instead — was ripping out monthly churn as a KPI entirely and replacing it with cohort retention curves. Not as a supplement. As a replacement.

Here's why.

## Monthly churn at low MRR is statistical noise wearing a suit

The math problem is obvious once you look at it, but nobody looks at it because ChartMogul and Baremetrics both put a big "Monthly Churn" number at the top of their dashboards and that number feels like a grade your teacher gave you.

If you have 50 customers and one cancels, your monthly churn is 2%. If two cancel, it's 4%. The difference between "healthy SaaS" and "you have a retention crisis" is literally one customer. One human being whose credit card expired and who never bothered to update it.

At 500 customers the signal-to-noise ratio improves. At 5,000 it's genuinely useful. At 50 it's a coin flip dressed up in a dashboard.

And yet — and this is the part that still makes me wince — every SaaS newsletter, every Indie Hackers post, every Twitter thread from a bootstrapper tells you to obsess over monthly churn from day one. Jason Lemkin has an entire SaaStr canon built around churn benchmarks. Those benchmarks (5% monthly is bad, 2% is good, under 1% is elite) were derived from companies doing eight figures in ARR. Applying them to your 38-customer micro-SaaS is like using a kitchen scale to weigh a feather.

I got this wrong for roughly six months. I'd tweak onboarding, re-run the numbers two weeks later, see churn had "dropped" from 4.1% to 2.8%, and pat myself on the back. The reality? I happened to have a quieter month. No onboarding change I made in that window was measurable in a sample of 47 people.

## What cohort retention actually shows you

Cohort retention is the boring answer everyone knows about and nobody under $20k MRR actually tracks. You group customers by the month they signed up. Then you watch what percentage of each group is still paying you 1, 2, 3, 6, 12 months later.

That's it. The whole concept.

What makes it powerful isn't the math — it's that it forces you to compare apples to apples. Your January 2024 cohort and your July 2024 cohort are two separate groups of humans who showed up for different reasons, probably through different channels, maybe after a different onboarding flow. Monthly churn smushes them all together. Cohort retention keeps them separate.

Here's what I saw the first time I actually built the chart (in a spreadsheet, badly, in April 2024):

- My November 2023 cohort had 82% retention at month 3
- My February 2024 cohort had 54% retention at month 3
- My blended "monthly churn" during this entire window was a placid 3.1%

February was a disaster. I'd launched a Product Hunt campaign, gotten a burst of 23 signups in a week, and those people churned hard because they weren't my ICP — they were Product Hunt tourists. Monthly churn couldn't see this because the November cohort was still paying reliably and masking the bleeding.

I would have kept doing Product Hunt launches. Cohort retention told me to stop.

## The three things cohort curves tell you that churn rate can't

First: where the cliff is. Most SaaS products have a drop-off point — month 2 for a lot of tools, month 6 for annual-ish usage patterns. If 40% of every cohort dies at month 2, you have an onboarding problem or an expectation-setting problem. You cannot see this in a blended monthly churn number. Ever.

Second: whether retention is improving over time. This is the one that matters most for product decisions. Is your March cohort retaining better than your January cohort at the same age? If yes, whatever you changed in February is working. If no, stop congratulating yourself on that new onboarding video.

Third: which acquisition channels bring customers who stick. Tag each cohort by dominant channel. My SEO-driven signups in Q1 2024 retained at 71% at month 6. My Twitter-driven signups retained at 34%. Same product. Same price. Wildly different humans. I shifted my effort accordingly and stopped feeling guilty about ignoring Twitter.

## Why nobody does this under $20k MRR

Two reasons, and they're both legitimate.

One, the tooling is genuinely annoying. Baremetrics' Startup plan is $129/mo, which is insane when you're doing $3k MRR. ChartMogul's free tier caps out fast and their paid plans start around $119/mo last I checked. ProfitWell (now Paddle) is free but requires connecting via their SDK and the cohort views are buried three clicks deep.

Two, building it yourself in a spreadsheet is a pain. You need to pull the Stripe API, bucket subscriptions by creation month, then track each subscription's status month over month. I did this the first time using a Python script and a Google Sheet and it took me a Saturday. It broke the next month when I added annual plans because my script assumed monthly billing cycles.

So most founders don't bother. They look at the big churn number their Stripe dashboard surfaces, feel okay or bad depending on what it says, and move on.

But here's the thing — and this is where I'll plant my flag — cohort retention is the single highest-ROI metric you can track before $50k MRR. Monthly churn becomes useful when your N is big enough that one cancellation doesn't swing the number. Cohort retention is useful at N=20. It's the only retention metric that's actually useful when you're small.

## How to build a cohort view without paying $129/mo

If you want to DIY it, here's the minimum viable approach I landed on after three iterations:

1. Export your Stripe subscriptions as CSV (or hit the API with a 20-line script)
2. Create a column for `cohort_month` = the month the subscription was created
3. For each month since, mark each subscription as active/canceled/paused
4. Pivot: cohort month on the Y axis, months-since-signup on the X axis, % still active in each cell
5. Look at the diagonal. Compare cohorts at the same age.

The first version I shipped was terrible — I forgot to handle pauses separately from cancellations, and I was counting refunded first-month customers as "retained then churned" instead of "never really customers." Fix those two things and you'll have something useful.

One quirk worth flagging: Stripe's `canceled_at` versus `ended_at` versus `current_period_end` will mess you up if you don't read the docs carefully. A customer who cancels on the 15th is usually still "active" until their period ends on the 30th. Decide how you want to count that and be consistent.

(If you're on annual plans mixed with monthly, normalize to monthly equivalents or build two separate cohort charts. Don't blend them. I learned this one the hard way in month nine.)

## What to actually do with the chart once you have it

Look at it once a month. Not once a day. The whole point of cohort retention is that it's a slow signal — you're waiting for cohorts to age so you can compare them.

The question you're asking is always: "is my newest complete cohort retaining better than my older ones at the same age?" If yes, keep doing whatever you're doing. If no, something regressed.

The second question is: "is there a specific month in the customer lifecycle where people bail?" If 30% of every cohort dies at month 2, that's your fix-it-now problem. Figure out why. Talk to the churned customers. Ship a change. Watch the next cohort.

> If your cohort curves are flat after month 3, you have a real business. If they keep declining, you have a leaky bucket with a subscription wrapper.

That's the only churn-related sentence I've ever seen that I actually believe.

## A note on the tooling I ended up using

I spent about a year bouncing between spreadsheets and the free tiers of various tools before I built [Stripe Revenue Tracker](#) — partly because I wanted cohort views without paying Baremetrics prices, and partly because I wanted to stop logging into Stripe 14 times a day to feel my MRR. It shows cohort retention by default on the home screen, which is the opposite of what Stripe's own dashboard does. If you want to roll your own with a Python script and a Sheet, genuinely do that — it's a useful exercise and you'll understand your data better for it. But if you've already done that twice and the script keeps breaking when Stripe ships a new subscription mode, you know where to find me.

The point isn't the tool. The point is: stop looking at monthly churn. Start looking at cohorts. Do it this week, even if it's ugly.
cover_image_prompt: Abstract editorial illustration, dark mode, deep navy and charcoal background with subtle teal and muted amber accents. Central composition: a series of horizontal descending curves resembling cohort retention lines, each starting at full opacity and fading into the void at different rates, layered on a faint isometric grid. One prominent line stays nearly flat while others collapse downward. Geometric, minimal, slightly risographic texture. Subtle grain. Premium tech magazine feel, reminiscent of Stripe Press or MIT Technology Review cover art. No text, no characters, no logos, no UI mockups.
